filing_and_agency_information.from_public_agency: City of Sacramento Department of Public Works

project_information.project_title: 24th Street Complete Streets and Safe Routes to School Improvements
project_information.project_applicant: City of Sacramento Department of Public Works
project_information.project_location_specific: 24th Street between Broadway and 2nd Avenue, including adjacent intersections at Castro Way, 1st Avenue, and 2nd Avenue, Sacramento, CA 95817
project_information.description_of_nature_purpose_and_benefi:
The project consists of minor transportation and streetscape improvements along approximately 0.6 miles of 24th Street in an existing, fully urbanized residential corridor in the City of Sacramento. The work includes: (1) installation of new high-visibility crosswalk striping and advance stop bars at four existing uncontrolled and controlled intersections; (2) construction of Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA)-compliant curb ramps at up to 18 existing corners where curb ramps are either missing or non-compliant; (3) narrowing of existing travel lanes and re-striping the roadway to provide buffered Class II bicycle lanes in each direction within the existing curb-to-curb width; (4) installation of new pedestrian-scale LED street lighting at key crossings in front of Elder Creek Elementary School and the nearby community center; (5) minor sidewalk infill to close two small gaps (each less than 75 linear feet) within the existing sidewalk network; and (6) associated minor utility adjustments to existing manholes, valve covers, and storm drain inlets entirely within the existing paved right-of-way.

The primary purpose of the project is to improve pedestrian and bicyclist safety and accessibility, particularly for school-aged children walking and biking to Elder Creek Elementary School and nearby bus stops, as well as for seniors and individuals with mobility impairments who rely on assistive devices. The project is intended to reduce vehicle speeds, increase driver awareness of crosswalks, and provide a more comfortable and predictable environment for non-motorized users without increasing roadway capacity or inducing additional vehicle traffic.

Beneficiaries of the project include: (a) approximately 480 students and their families who attend Elder Creek Elementary School and currently walk or bike along 24th Street; (b) local residents living in the Oak Park neighborhood who rely on walking, bicycling, and transit for daily trips; (c) transit riders using Sacramento Regional Transit bus routes that run along Broadway and intersect with 24th Street; (d) people with disabilities who will gain safer, ADA-compliant curb ramps and smoother sidewalk connections; and (e) the broader community through anticipated reductions in collisions, near-misses, and unsafe mid-block crossings. The project does not expand the existing roadway footprint, does not add new through vehicle lanes, and is limited to minor alterations and safety enhancements of existing public facilities.
project_information.name_of_person_or_agency_carrying_out_pr: City of Sacramento Department of Public Works, Engineering Services Division

exemption_status.categorical_exemption_state_type_and_sec: Class 1, Existing Facilities (Section 15301(c)); Class 4, Minor Alterations to Land (Section 15304(h))
exemption_status.reasons_why_project_is_exempt:
The project is exempt from the California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA) pursuant to CEQA Guidelines Section 15301, Class 1 (Existing Facilities), and Section 15304, Class 4 (Minor Alterations to Land). The project involves minor alterations to existing public streets, sidewalks, and appurtenant facilities with negligible or no expansion of use.

Under Section 15301(c), the exemption applies to the operation, repair, maintenance, permitting, leasing, licensing, or minor alteration of existing public or private structures and facilities, including existing highways and streets, sidewalks, gutters, bicycle and pedestrian trails, and similar facilities, involving negligible or no expansion of existing or former use. This project consists primarily of: restriping an existing roadway within the current curb-to-curb width to add buffered bicycle lanes; installing high-visibility crosswalk striping and advance stop bars at existing intersections; replacing non-compliant curb ramps with ADA-compliant curb ramps at existing corners; adding pedestrian-scale lighting at existing poles or new poles within the existing right-of-way; and making minor utility adjustments. No new travel lanes are added, no roadway widening beyond existing curbs will occur, and no new vehicle capacity is created. The function of 24th Street as a two-lane local collector street remains unchanged.

Under Section 15304(h), the exemption applies to minor alterations in land, water, and vegetation, including the creation of bicycle lanes on existing rights-of-way. The project’s reconfiguration of striping to provide buffered on-street bicycle lanes in both directions is a textbook example of work contemplated by this exemption. All work will take place in previously disturbed, paved, and urbanized areas, and there will be no grading of undisturbed land, no removal of mature trees, and no work in waterways or sensitive habitats.

The project area is located in a fully built-out residential neighborhood in central Sacramento characterized by one- and two-story houses, existing sidewalks, curb and gutter, overhead and underground utilities, and existing street lighting. There are no known wetlands, riparian areas, or other sensitive biological resources within the project footprint. A records search and review of the City’s GIS layers indicate that the project is not located on or adjacent to any listed historic resources, and work is limited to surface-level improvements in previously disturbed rights-of-way, making the potential to adversely affect historical or archaeological resources extremely low.

The project does not trigger any of the exceptions to the use of categorical exemptions listed in CEQA Guidelines Section 15300.2. Specifically: (a) the project is not located in a designated environmentally sensitive area; (b) there are no unusual circumstances that would distinguish this project from typical complete streets or safe routes to school improvements, and there is no evidence that such circumstances could result in a significant environmental effect; (c) the project will not result in damage to scenic resources within a state scenic highway; (d) the project site is not on any list of hazardous waste sites compiled pursuant to Government Code Section 65962.5; and (e) the project does not involve, nor is it located within the vicinity of, any historical resources as defined in CEQA Guidelines Section 15064.5.

Traffic operations analyses performed as part of the project scoping confirmed that the re-striping and addition of bicycle lanes will not result in significant traffic impacts or increased congestion beyond existing conditions. Vehicle speeds are expected to decrease modestly due to lane narrowing and increased driver awareness of crosswalks, which is a desired safety outcome. Noise and air quality impacts will be temporary and limited to construction activities over an anticipated 8–10 week construction period, with standard best management practices employed (e.g., limiting construction hours, controlling dust, maintaining equipment in good working order). These short-term construction effects do not rise to the level of significant environmental impacts under CEQA.

For these reasons, the project is appropriately classified as categorically exempt from CEQA under Class 1 (Section 15301(c)) and Class 4 (Section 15304(h)), and none of the exceptions to categorical exemptions apply.

lead_agency_contact.contact_person: Melissa R. Nguyen, Senior Civil Engineer
